assumptions: The question is about hedge accounting in consolidated financial statements under IFRS 9.
A declared intragroup dividend that has been recorded as a receivable/payable is treated as an intragroup monetary item.
The entities involved may have different functional currencies; otherwise the specific foreign exchange hedging issue would not arise.

approaches.label: General external-party requirement
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, IFRS 9 generally limits hedged items to assets, liabilities, firm commitments or forecast transactions with external parties. Intragroup items are not eligible unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: An intragroup dividend receivable cannot be designated under the general rule alone.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 says intragroup items are generally not hedged items in consolidated financial statements.

approaches.label: Intragroup monetary item exception
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 expressly allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to be a hedged item in consolidated financial statements if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A booked dividend receivable/payable can fall into this route under the stated assumption.
conditions: The dividend has been recognised as an intragroup receivable/payable
The receivable/payable is denominated in a currency creating foreign exchange exposure
The FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedging relationship is formally designated and documented at inception
The IFRS 9 hedge effectiveness criteria are met
approaches.practical_implication: A formally documented hedge relationship at consolidated level is possible if the dividend receivable qualifies as an intragroup monetary item with non-eliminated FX exposure.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits FX risk on intragroup monetary items as hedged items when consolidation does not fully eliminate the exposure.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness for hedge accounting.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction exception
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 separately permits FX risk on a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction only if the transaction is in a non-functional currency and the FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. That route does not fit a dividend already recorded as a receivable.
conditions: Transaction is still forecast and highly probable
Transaction is denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the entity entering into it
The FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss
approaches.practical_implication: If the dividend has already been recognised as a receivable, this is not the relevant hedge-accounting route.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows certain forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged in consolidation.
Paragraph B6.3.5 explains that many intragroup payments do not qualify unless their FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 applies only to hedges of net investments in foreign operations, where the hedged item is an amount of net assets. It should not be applied by analogy to other hedge-accounting situations such as an intragroup dividend receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: Net investment hedge guidance should not be used to support hedging the dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 7–8 limit the Interpretation to net investment hedges and prohibit analogy to other hedge types.
Paragraphs 11–12 describe the hedged item as net assets of a foreign operation, not an intragroup receivable.
